extra.reasoning:
The property requires that in every reachable state, opted_out=true implies sent=false. Transition [t5] directly breaks this: on send_attempt with guard (opted_out and ticks>=4), it sets sent:=true, producing a state where opted_out=true AND sent=true.

Counterexample execution (6 events, within the 8-event bound):
1. optout: [t1] guard true -> opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0
2. tick: [t4] ticks<4 -> ticks=1, sent=false
3. tick: [t4] -> ticks=2, sent=false
4. tick: [t4] -> ticks=3, sent=false
5. tick: [t4] -> ticks=4, sent=false
6. send_attempt: [t5] guard (opted_out=true and ticks=4>=4) holds -> sent:=true

Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=4. This violates globally(opted_out implies not sent).

Note on rule interaction: on send_attempt with opted_out=true, both [t3] (guard opted_out) and [t5] (guard opted_out and ticks>=4) are enabled. No priority is specified, so an execution in which [t5] fires is reachable. Since the property must hold on every reachable execution, the existence of this [t5]-firing execution suffices to violate it. The system contains a logical flaw: t5 grants a send capability to opted-out subjects once enough ticks accumulate, contradicting the intent that opted-out subjects never get sent.
